name: Sereno
idea: Sereno is a gentle habit and recovery companion for remote knowledge workers at risk of burnout. It blends short science-based micro-habits (movement, light exposure, focus blocks, wind-down routines) with an AI coach that adapts the daily plan to the user's calendar load and self-reported energy — nudging without nagging, and measuring recovery instead of streaks.
problem: Remote workers stack back-to-back video calls with no natural breaks, and existing habit apps make it worse by punishing broken streaks and ignoring context. People don't need another gamified checklist; they need a plan that shrinks on heavy days, grows on light ones, and treats rest as progress — otherwise they abandon the app within two weeks and keep sliding toward burnout.
company_context: We are a husband-and-wife team: a former occupational-health physician who spent a decade running corporate wellness programs, and a mobile engineer who shipped two top-100 productivity apps. After the physician's own burnout leave in 2024, we started building Sereno around the clinical recovery protocols she used with patients — turned into something people can actually follow between meetings.
pain_points: Habit apps punish broken streaks, which demotivates exactly the people who need them most
Daily plans ignore real calendar load, so busy days make the app feel naive and heavy days get skipped entirely
Wellness advice is generic and not grounded in the user's actual energy and recovery signals

target_users.label: Remote Knowledge Workers
target_users.description: Individual contributors and managers working 6+ hours/day on video calls who feel early burnout symptoms but won't book a clinic visit

target_users.label: People-Ops / Wellness Leads
target_users.description: HR and people-operations leads at 50-500 person remote companies looking for a wellness benefit employees will actually keep using

objectives: Reach 8-week retention above 40% (vs the ~10% habit-app norm)
Show a measurable drop in self-reported exhaustion within 4 weeks of use
Land 20 company wellness-benefit pilots in the first year

audience.notes: Clinical experience from a decade of corporate wellness programs, plus 60 user interviews with remote workers; retention benchmarks from published habit-app research.

competitive_landscape: Headspace and Calm own meditation but not adaptive daily planning; Fabulous and Habitica are streak-driven gamification (the exact pattern we reject); corporate platforms like Wellhub aggregate gyms rather than build daily behavior. Nobody adapts the plan to calendar load — that context-awareness is the differentiator.
business_constraints: Health-adjacent but NOT a medical device — copy must avoid diagnostic claims (regulatory line). Two-person team; mobile-first (iOS first, Android in month 4). B2C subscription plus B2B pilot pricing; calendar integration limited to read-only Google/Microsoft OAuth scopes.

brand.logo_usage: Use the rounded hexagonal S mark generously padded on soft backgrounds for app icon and onboarding; in-app headers use it small and quiet in a corner — the interface should breathe, the logo should never shout.
brand.brand_voice: Warm, calm, and permission-giving — like a good physiotherapist: encouraging without cheerleading, honest about setbacks, never guilt-tripping. Rest days are framed as progress.
brand.design_notes: Soft organic pastels (sage, peach, lavender) on warm off-white; rounded corners everywhere, botanical illustration accents, no red anywhere in the palette. Progress is shown as gentle fills, never as breakable streaks or alarms.
